ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general deer meat food product class (FOODON_03311349); no specific stir-fried venison with ginger and spring onions entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers venison as a raw meat category. No specific stir-fried preparation code exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226934003 identifies venison as a substance; no stir-fried preparation concept exists. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of stir-fried venison with ginger and spring onions is approximately 250 g, representing a shared zi char dish portion for one to two persons.

nutrient_source.title: Venison, cooked, roasted (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 17175) adapted with stir-fry cooking adjustment for oil and condiment additions
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base nutrient values derived from USDA FoodData Central cooked venison (roasted, NDB 17175 / FDC ID 174299). Values adjusted upward for fat and sodium to account for stir-fry cooking oil (~5 g oil per 100 g dish), soy sauce, and oyster sauce additions typical of Malaysian/Singaporean zi char preparation. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors. This is an estimated composite; no exact match for this dish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — meat and protein-dominant mixed dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for venison stir-fried with ginger and spring onions. GI estimated at 15 based on analogous protein-dominant stir-fried meat dishes with minimal carbohydrate content. Protein-dominant dishes with low carbohydrate content consistently yield GI values in the 10–20 range per University of Sydney GI Database methodology. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: (15 × 7.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.1 (rounded), where available carbohydrate = 2.8 g/100 g × 250 g serving = 7.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce (~480 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced soy sauce when ordering, as a full 250 g restaurant serving delivers approximately 1,200 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~15) and low carbohydrate content (2.8 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Venison stir-fried with ginger and spring onions is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low GI of approximately 15 and minimal carbohydrate load; the high protein content (22.5 g/100 g) supports satiety and glycemic stability, though sodium from condiments should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (2.1 g/100 g) and elevated sodium from condiments
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Venison is a lean game meat with a favourable unsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio and is rich in iron and B12; however, the sodium contributed by soy sauce and oyster sauce in stir-fry preparation elevates cardiovascular risk, and individuals with cardiovascular disease should request low-sodium condiments and limit portion size to 150 g per meal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Venison Stir-Fried with Ginger and Spring Onions is a Malaysian and Singaporean zi char restaurant dish featuring tender slices of deer meat (venison) wok-fried at high heat with fresh ginger, spring onions (scallions), garlic, soy sauce, and oyster sauce, finished with a drizzle of sesame oil. The dish is characterised by a savoury, slightly pungent aroma from the ginger, a tender and slightly chewy texture from the venison, and a glossy, umami-rich sauce. Venison is a lean game meat that is lower in total fat and saturated fat than beef or pork, while being rich in high-quality protein (approximately 22.5 g per 100 g), iron (3.8 mg/100 g), zinc (3.2 mg/100 g), and vitamin B12 (2.8 mcg/100 g). The glycemic index is very low (estimated ~15), making this dish glycemically favourable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The primary nutritional concern is sodium, which is elevated due to soy sauce and oyster sauce used in zi char-style cooking, reaching approximately 480 mg per 100 g. This dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans, and contains soy and shellfish allergens from the condiments. It is typically served as a shared main course in zi char restaurants alongside steamed rice and vegetables. The dish is not halal-certified in most Singapore zi char establishments unless specifically stated. Clinically, it is a protein-dense, low-GI option that supports muscle maintenance and glycemic control, but requires sodium monitoring for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease.

confidence_reason: No exact match for this dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked venison (FDC ID 174299) with estimated adjustments for stir-fry oil and condiment additions. GI is estimated by analogy from protein-dominant stir-fried meat dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the base venison nutrient data is from a high-quality approved source and the stir-fry adjustments are methodologically defensible.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked venison (FDC ID 174299) and is not reliably estimated for this preparation. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from USDA venison data and adjusted proportionally for added cooking oil. Halal status is marked false as venison availability and halal certification in Singapore zi char establishments is not guaranteed without explicit certification. The dish is served at Marina Seafood House — Boat Quay on field survey data at SGD 24.00. Sodium value of 480 mg/100 g reflects estimated contribution from soy sauce and oyster sauce in a typical zi char stir-fry preparation; actual restaurant sodium may vary. GI set to 15 (low) based on analogous protein-dominant stir-fried meat dish estimation per University of Sydney GI Database methodology; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in the literature.
